100 - How I Won Productivity (As A Sales Rep) - YouTube.mp4
Are you tired of working 60, 70, 80 hours a week, not making that much money, and then you see Clive over here working 30 or 40 hours a week? Two to three times the sales you are, well— if that's you, I'm gonna show you why Clive is kicking your butt and show you how to kick his butt. How do you win at the Productivity game as a salesperson, sales professional? Doesn't matter if you sell B2C, B2B, door-to-door, B2G. It's all the same. The first thing that you want to focus on, regardless of what you sell, is what we call income-producing activities. All right, let me repeat that income-producing activities. Now, what I always saw the Clive do, all right, in my example, because I would say I was probably the Clive, is I was only focused on income-producing activities.
Where I would notice that 99% of the other salespeople in the offices that I sold at they were on their phones looking at social media and email, would pop up every 10 minutes. They would stop what they were doing, stop their cold calling, or stop whatever they were doing, go to their email, respond to the email, then come back to cold calling. Were like little squirrels, like just no. I would say they were like leafs. Just the wind would blow in and they would just kind of blow out all over the place and they had no structure to their day. And they'd end up working 60, 70, 80 hours a week and not making any money. And I'm like, that's not smart. So you want to be like Clive.
So I'm going to show you how to be like Clive, which in this example was me as a sales professional. So I only focused on income-producing activities. Because if you're a salesperson, where do you make money? When you're talking to prospects, right? Because you make commissions. So, if you're not talking to prospects, or maybe even like, you know, getting ready, sold B2B, like getting ready to present in front of a boardroom of decision makers, like actually going over what you're going to say and what you're going to ask, you were not focused on income-producing activities, all right? I would notice that most of the salespeople that worked in the companies I worked for, they would, every time an email would come in, they would get distracted, they would respond.
To that email, then they would go back to cold calling or calling outbound leads. And another email comes in ten minutes later, they would get off the phone, go back to that email, and back and forth. They pick up their phone, you know, look at their Facebook or whatever, you know, for 15 minutes, and they would go to the bathroom. Then, after the bathroom, they would come into the break room and there would be, like, all the broke salespeople that would like, you know, blame the company, blame the leads, the product sucks, my prospects are always broke, help me, and they would sit there and drink their coffee for another 20 minutes. Then they, I mean, they just waste all that time, and they thought they were working 10, 12, 14 hours a day.
But if you really went over how many hours they were doing this, you'd be shocked. It was maybe two to three, maybe four hours a day out of that 12-hour a day. And they would look at me because I'd work like seven hours, sometimes eight. And I was making, well. Do you want me to tell you? I was making multiple six figures a month in commissions. Let me repeat that. Multiple six figures a month in commissions. Grand a month in commissions and most of them are making three, four, five, maybe seven or eight grand a month if they were lucky working twice as many hours. How do we do it? All right. So I'm going to show you income-producing activities. So what I would do is I focused on a technique, okay?
And a lot of people talk about this. I've seen even the other sales trainers talk about this. About it is called the PTB. It's prospect time blocking. Okay, so what I mean my prospect time blocking is I'd have certain hours in the day where I would call. Okay, and then, right after that hour was done, when I got off the phone with that last prospect, then I might have the next hour I was calling outbound leads. People who had responded to some social media, that leads had come in. Okay, name, email, phone number. Now in the companies that I work for, that I'm giving an example of, we didn't have many of those. It was mainly prospecting, like cold outreach.
Alright, so if you're a salesperson that only gets outbound leads—name, email, phone number— you would prospect time block times in the day to call those leads, all right. So I had my They structured 100% seven to eight hours straight. Hardly any breaks because I was focused on producing activity. So prospect time blocking. Now, I even had a little sign on my door. It was like those little door hangers, you know, the things you have on the hotels that says 'Do not disturb.' And it said... 'Prospect.' Time block. Do not come in unless 9-1-1. And nobody would knock on my office door because I knew I was actually working, all right? Now. So let me give you an example. Let's say from...
In the morning, I would have times where I would check email so I'd always check my emails three times a day. I would check it usually around seven in the morning. Okay, for about 10– 15 minutes. Then I would have a about a 15-minute break, usually right before I had my lunch break, which usually my lunch break, I'd take about 10 or 15 minutes because why would I take an hour off income-producing activities? I make no money doing that. That's a whole other story. So about 11:45 in the morning, I checked my emails again, and then I would check it once again before I left the office, maybe 4, 4:35, depending on when I was leaving. There might be some days I worked even longer, maybe 6.
I'm always going to check it before I leave. Now, that being said, if there was a really really important email that came in prospecting back, we're ready to go call me back in two minutes. I had an assistant. Yes, when you get really good at sales, you can hire an assistant. Actually, that company hired me an assistant, and my assistant would come in, 'Hey, you got an email from XYZ, make sure you get back to him,' and then I would obviously change my schedule to do that, all right? So I'm not saying you never check your emails, but... you know. Just prospect, just block it off, all right? So you're not checking every five minutes when an email comes in. You waste so much time, especially on social media, right?
So make sure you schedule your day. Let's say, you know, and it depends on what you sell. You know, maybe from 8 to 10 in the morning. If you're in an industry where you cold call, that's what you're doing— cold calling. Maybe from 10 to 11, you're getting back with prospects that didn't buy. From 12 to 2, you've now scheduled meetings with inbound, you know, with leads that you had called, cold called, that then scheduled on your calendar, right? On what you sell or maybe if you did inbound leads you've got a certain part of the day that's scheduled only for inbound, and then three hours a day you're cold calling or you're calling back people who didn't buy. Okay. What I'm suggesting is you just block off the times it's best for you.
And if you stick to that structure, it's almost like a factory. It's like just—factory, factory, factory. It's like you're putting together the Mercedes and it just spits out good things, lots of sales, lots of prospects that are helped. So focus on prospect time block. Now, like I said, there's going to be times when you're time blocking where you're going to call outbound. You might even be lucky if you're an industry that has inbound leads. A couple of the industries I sold in, I'd usually... get maybe 30% inbound and let's say maybe another 30% outbound, and then the, you know, maybe the other 40% was just cold calling that I would bring in. Qualify and then book on my calendar right now. You can also focus on referrals.
So I had times during the day where I was calling back clients who had just had their problem solved. And once again, that's more industry specific. But let's say if you sold, I don't know, cybersecurity to banks, you'd want to make sure to get referrals that you... Call after the problem was solved not like 'right' but 'before' so you might want to call that prospect back and find out kind of what's going on. Stay in touch, what's going on, and then ask for a furrow. So I'd have times during the week where I would call back clients to get referrals. I made, probably, I'm going to go out on a limb here. I could be off, but I bet probably 20% of all my sales came from calling back clients who would refer me to other people.
And that's a whole other training that I can do. Can do for you another time, in fact, you know. If you want to learn how to do referrals, you can actually go to our free Facebook group, Sales Revolution dot Pro, and ask for referrals in there. We can message it over to you. But we do a lot more industry-specific training in that Facebook group, so you're welcome to go there if you want to learn how to get better referrals, because I made a lot of money from that. Now, you're going to have times during that day where you're going to have times for, and I hate this word in sales, following up with people, prospects who did not buy. Maybe six months ago, a year ago, two months ago.
Now, when you call them, don't say, 'Hey, John, it's Jeremy Miner.' I'm just following up with you or I'm just checking in. Why do you not want to say those words? Words? Because every salesperson says that to them, that calls them or emails them. How many emails do you get from people listening to you where they're like, 'I'm just checking in.' 'I'm just following up.' And after you read those words, you... Don't read anymore. Be honest with yourself. You know what I mean. I get those every day. So don't use the words 'following up' or 'checking in' because every salesperson that's also trying to sell them is also using those words. And if you use the same words, like every other salesperson that they don't like, guess how they're going to view you?
Probably not in a good way. Okay. Just getting back to you, or I just had time to get back to you, or I'm just getting back to you. There's different words. You can rephrase that. All right. So the biggest point here is focus on income-producing activities. Okay. If you want to get on Facebook or Instagram or YouTube or whatever, my suggestion is to do that after work because, during work, if you want to make a lot of money, you want to focus on actually making money. Alright, so maybe if you're going to the bathroom, you can set down the toilet, then you can get on your phone and Facebook. You can actually prospect time block that I'm just joking. You know what I mean. So focus on income-producing activities.
Stay away from email only three times a day, unless it's an emergency email that your company tells you about, okay, or you get good. Get an assistant and they'll show you that. And then stay away from checking every email every 10 minutes. Stay away from the company. I always call it the company club. Negative salespeople that once you go lunch and then you stay up to lunch for 90 minutes to two hours— I always said no. I always brought my lunch and I always ate my lunch at my desk, which would take me about 10 minutes. Then I was back to what income-producing activities. So what I would do is I would work that seven to eight hours a day, while everybody else was working like 10 to 12.
I was making like 20 times more. All right. So it's not just about, you know, time blocking and income-present activities. It's what questions are you asking? How are you using your tonality? How are you getting to lower their guard? How are you seeding certain things that you say and ask that actually prevent? Most of the objections you lose deals from, then if you do get a concern here and there, how do you help the prospect overcome it? But it's a combination of focusing on income-producing activities, and actually your sales ability, and you do that. You can work far less. Okay, there were some times on Fridays. where I would come into the office and I would leave at noon. And guess what? You think my sales manager or the VP of sales or even the chief sales officer cared?
If Jay Miner left at noon on a Friday, when I was making them. tens of millions of dollars in sales every month. Now, you can have a lot of flexibility when you're the top salesperson in your company, in the entire industry. How do you get there? You're not born with it, right? You have to acquire those skills, right? So focus on income-producing activities and acquiring a much more advanced sales ability, and you're going to do exceptionally better than you are right now, and you're going to have a lot more fun and work less hours. There you go.
